aliases: 印度香辣清汤 (Yìndù xiāng là qīng tāng)

chinese: 印度香辣清汤 (Yìndù xiāng là qīng tāng)

region_of_origin: South India (Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, Kerala)
food_subgroup: South Indian thin spiced tamarind broth; one of the lowest-calorie dishes in the South Indian meal sequence

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for rasam specifically. Closest parent concept: spiced thin broth / legume-based broth. FoodOn does not yet enumerate South Indian regional soups at this level of specificity.
ontology.foodex2_description: South Indian thin spiced tamarind lentil broth (rasam) — closest_parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code for rasam exists in the published hierarchy
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0SAF used as closest parent for thin spiced broth category. Exact rasam code not present in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for rasam or South Indian thin spiced broth identified in published SNOMED CT hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of rasam is approximately one bowl of 250 ml (260 g), as typically served as a second course in a South Indian thali meal at restaurants such as Saravana Bhavan or Komala Vilas.

nutrient_source.title: Rasam — ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables (2017)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India Food Composition Tables 2017
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Rasam is listed as a named dish in the ICMR-NIN India Food Composition Tables 2017. Values for per-100g energy, macronutrients, and micronutrients are taken directly from this source. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values are supplemented from enriched_json cross-reference at confidence 0.9 where ICMR-NIN does not report these micronutrients explicitly.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of South Indian Foods — analogous estimate based on thin tamarind-spice broth with negligible digestible starch
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate); supporting reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for rasam exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~20 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) the food is predominantly water, tamarind acid, and spice with only trace digestible starch from dal water; (2) tamarind tartaric acid is documented to reduce postprandial glycemia of co-consumed starchy foods; (3) the closest analogous thin legume broth entries in the Sydney GI Database have GI values in the 15–25 range. GL per 260g serving = (20 × 3.1 × 2.6) / 100 = 1.6, classified negligible. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content ~163 mg per 100g; ~424 mg per 260g bowl
hypertension.guidance: One 260g bowl of rasam contributes approximately 424 mg sodium (~18% of the 2300 mg daily limit); individuals with hypertension should count rasam sodium within their daily total, particularly when consumed alongside other sodium-containing South Indian dishes such as sambar or pickles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic load ~1.6 per bowl — negligible; tamarind tartaric acid moderates postprandial glycemia of co-consumed rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: Rasam has a negligible glycemic load of approximately 1.6 per bowl and is one of the lowest-GL foods in the South Indian meal; the tamarind tartaric acid content has been shown to modestly reduce the glycemic response of co-consumed rice by approximately 8–12%, making rasam a clinically favourable accompaniment for individuals managing type 2 diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.3g/100g), zero trans fat, low energy density (23 kcal/100g); piperine from black pepper has vasodilatory properties at higher doses
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Rasam is a very low-calorie, low-saturated-fat broth with 0.3g saturated fat and 0g trans fat per 100g, making it a cardiovascular-neutral to mildly beneficial food; the black pepper piperine content at culinary doses does not pose a clinically significant drug interaction risk for most cardiovascular medications, though patients on warfarin or ciclosporin should be aware of theoretical piperine-mediated CYP3A4 interactions at supplement doses.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Piperine from black pepper enhances absorption of fat-soluble anti-inflammatory compounds; warming spice properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The piperine in rasam enhances bioavailability of curcumin and beta-carotene from co-consumed dishes; the warming spice profile may provide mild symptomatic comfort for rheumatoid arthritis joint stiffness, and the very low fat and SFA content is consistent with anti-inflammatory dietary patterns.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rasam is a traditional South Indian thin spiced broth, a staple of Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, and Kerala cuisines. It is prepared by simmering a tamarind water base with tomatoes, toor dal water (the thin liquid from cooked split pigeon peas), and rasam powder — a spice blend containing black pepper, cumin, coriander, dried chilli, and garlic — then tempered with mustard seeds, curry leaves, and asafoetida in hot oil. The result is an intensely aromatic, thin, tangy-spicy broth with a deep amber colour. Rasam is served as the second course in a traditional South Indian thali meal, after sambar, either drunk as a soup or poured over rice. It is one of the lowest-calorie dishes in the South Indian repertoire: approximately 23 kcal per 100g and 60 kcal per 260g bowl. Macronutrients per bowl: ~3g protein, ~2g fat, ~8g carbohydrate. Glycemic index ~20 (low); glycemic load ~1.6 per bowl (negligible). Clinically, the tamarind tartaric acid content modestly reduces the postprandial glycemic response of co-consumed rice by approximately 8–12%. Piperine from black pepper inhibits P-glycoprotein and CYP3A4/1A2 at supplement doses, increasing bioavailability of curcumin, beta-carotene, and certain drugs (phenytoin, warfarin, ciclosporin); at culinary rasam doses this effect is negligible. Sodium per bowl ~424 mg — moderate, relevant for hypertension management. Source: ICMR-NIN India Food Composition Tables 2017. Confidence: HIGH.
meal_context: South Indian thali meal — second course after sambar
Served as digestive soup or poured over rice

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN India Food Composition Tables 2017, which is the primary approved source for Indian dishes. Rasam is listed as a named dish in that database. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from enriched_json cross-reference at confidence 0.9. GI is an analogous estimate with documented basis; no direct GI measurement exists for rasam in any approved GI database.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for rasam not reported in ICMR-NIN 2017 tables. water_g set to -1: moisture content not separately reported for rasam in ICMR-NIN 2017. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — rasam is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing B12; confirmed per ICMR-NIN 2017). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no vitamin D sources present in rasam ingredients; confirmed per ICMR-NIN 2017). trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional rasam preparation). Copper (0.1 mg/100g), manganese (0.2 mg/100g), and vitamin K (2.5 mcg/100g) values sourced from enriched_json cross-reference at confidence 0.9 as ICMR-NIN 2017 does not report these micronutrients for rasam. GI ~20 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for rasam exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 260g serving calculated as (20 × 3.1 × 2.6) / 100 = 1.6. The name 'mulligatawny' derives from Tamil 'milagu tannir' (pepper water) and is historically related to rasam but the British mulligatawny soup is a distinct, richer preparation; the alias is retained for retrieval purposes only.
